\documentclass[conference]{IEEEtran}

\begin{document}

\title{Outlier-Aware CIM Mapping for Large Language Model Inference:\\
Characterization, Mitigation, and Hardware Co-Design}

\author{
\IEEEauthorblockN{Anonymous Authors}
\IEEEauthorblockA{Anonymous Institution}
}

\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Compute-in-Memory (CIM) architectures offer significant energy and throughput advantages for large language model (LLM) inference by performing matrix-vector multiplications directly in the memory array. However, LLMs exhibit severe activation outliers—certain channels with magnitudes orders-of-magnitude larger than typical values—that cause frequent ADC saturation and substantially degrade inference accuracy. In this paper, we present a systematic characterization of activation outliers in LLM transformer layers on CIM arrays, revealing that outlier-driven ADC saturation affects up to 98\% of MAC outputs at 7-bit ADC resolution. To mitigate this, we propose CIM-Aware SmoothQuant (CIM-SQ), a hardware-software co-design approach that adapts the SmoothQuant technique to account for CIM-specific constraints including memristor weight range and DAC resolution. CIM-SQ optimizes per-layer migration strength $\alpha$ to simultaneously minimize activation quantization difficulty and respect physical hardware limits. Evaluated on GPT-2 with WikiText-2, CIM-SQ achieves consistent perplexity improvements over the unmitigated CIM baseline across ADC bit configurations from 7 to 10 bits, enabling more hardware-efficient CIM deployment of LLMs.
\end{abstract}

\begin{IEEEkeywords}
Compute-in-Memory, Large Language Models, Quantization, SmoothQuant, ADC Saturation, Outlier Mitigation
\end{IEEEkeywords}

%────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}

Large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-2~\cite{radford2019language} and the OPT family~\cite{zhang2022opt} have achieved remarkable performance across natural language tasks, but their deployment is bottlenecked by the massive memory bandwidth and energy demands of repeated large matrix-vector multiplications (MVM). Compute-in-Memory (CIM) architectures~\cite{chi2016prime,shafiee2016isaac} address this bottleneck by storing neural network weights directly in non-volatile memory cells (e.g., RRAM or PCM) and performing MVM operations in-situ, dramatically reducing data movement.

Despite their promise, CIM arrays face a critical challenge when applied to LLM inference: \textbf{activation outliers}. As first reported by Dettmers et al.~\cite{dettmers2022llmint8} and further analyzed by Xiao et al.~\cite{xiao2023smoothquant}, LLM transformer activations contain systematic outlier channels whose magnitudes can be $10\times$--$100\times$ larger than the median. In a CIM context, these outliers cause the analog MAC accumulation to exceed the ADC full-scale range, resulting in output saturation and severe accuracy degradation.

Existing outlier mitigation approaches such as LLM.int8()~\cite{dettmers2022llmint8} and SmoothQuant~\cite{xiao2023smoothquant} were designed for digital quantization engines and do not account for CIM-specific hardware constraints:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Memristor weight range}: The conductance ratio of ON/OFF states limits effective weight precision and dynamic range.
    \item \textbf{DAC resolution}: Input activations are applied via a DAC; the input quantization step size affects MAC accuracy.
    \item \textbf{Fixed ADC range}: The ADC full-scale is a hardware parameter determined at design time; it cannot adapt to per-token outliers at runtime.
\end{itemize}

In this paper, we make the following contributions:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Outlier Characterization on CIM}: We provide the first systematic per-layer analysis of LLM activation outliers in the context of CIM ADC saturation, quantifying saturation rates across ADC bit widths (3--11 bits).

    \item \textbf{CIM-Aware SmoothQuant (CIM-SQ)}: We adapt SmoothQuant's smooth scaling framework to CIM hardware constraints, formulating per-layer alpha optimization that balances activation difficulty reduction against memristor dynamic range and DAC precision limits.

    \item \textbf{Hardware-Software Pareto Analysis}: We demonstrate that CIM-SQ achieves lower perplexity than unmitigated CIM inference across multiple ADC bit configurations, enabling hardware designers to select a more area-efficient (lower-bit) ADC while maintaining accuracy targets.
\end{enumerate}

%────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Background}
\label{sec:background}

\subsection{Compute-In-Memory for LLM Inference}

CIM arrays implement MVM as $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{W}$ where weights $\mathbf{W}$ are stored as conductance states in a crossbar array. Input vector $\mathbf{x}$ is applied via row-wise DACs, current accumulates along columns (Kirchhoff's current law), and the analog output is digitized by column-wise ADCs. This eliminates the memory access energy of conventional digital accelerators.

For LLM transformer layers, the dominant operations are the attention projection ($\mathbf{x} \cdot W_{QKV}$) and feed-forward network projections ($\mathbf{x} \cdot W_{FC1}$, $\mathbf{h} \cdot W_{FC2}$). GPT-2 uses \texttt{Conv1D} layers with weight shape $[C_{in}, C_{out}]$, requiring careful handling distinct from standard \texttt{nn.Linear} ($[C_{out}, C_{in}]$).

\subsection{Activation Outliers in LLMs}

Starting from the sixth transformer layer of LLMs, activation tensors develop systematic \emph{outlier channels}: a small fraction of feature dimensions (typically 0.1--1\%) that consistently carry values $10\times$--$100\times$ larger than other channels~\cite{dettmers2022llmint8}. These outliers are structured (same channels across all tokens) and persistent across inputs.

In CIM, outlier channels cause MAC outputs to exceed the ADC full-scale $V_{FS}$:
\begin{equation}
    y_j = \sum_{i} x_i \cdot w_{ij} > V_{FS} \Rightarrow \text{ADC saturation}
    \label{eq:saturation}
\end{equation}
Unlike digital quantization where one can widen the representation, CIM ADC range is physically fixed by the circuit area budget.

\subsection{SmoothQuant}

SmoothQuant~\cite{xiao2023smoothquant} addresses outliers by introducing a per-channel smooth scaling factor $\mathbf{s}$:
\begin{equation}
    \mathbf{y} = (\mathbf{x} \cdot \text{diag}(\mathbf{s})^{-1}) \cdot (\text{diag}(\mathbf{s}) \cdot \mathbf{W})
    \label{eq:sq}
\end{equation}
The modified activation $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} / \mathbf{s}$ and weight $\hat{\mathbf{W}} = \text{diag}(\mathbf{s}) \cdot \mathbf{W}$ preserve the mathematical output while redistributing difficulty from activations to weights. The migration strength $\alpha$ controls $\mathbf{s}_j = \max(|\mathbf{x}_j|)^\alpha / \max(|\mathbf{w}_j|)^{1-\alpha}$.

%────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Outlier Characterization on CIM Arrays}
\label{sec:characterization}

\subsection{Methodology}

We measure per-layer activation statistics using forward hooks on GPT-2's 49 linear-like layers (48 \texttt{Conv1D} + 1 \texttt{lm\_head}) evaluated on WikiText-2 (512-token sequences, 32 calibration batches). For each layer, we record:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Per-channel activation magnitude statistics (mean, max, outlier fraction $> 6\sigma$)
    \item ADC saturation rate: fraction of MAC outputs exceeding the ADC full-scale at various bit widths
    \item Extra ADC bits required to avoid saturation (overhead analysis)
\end{itemize}

The ADC full-scale is calibrated as the mean per-batch maximum MAC output, then the saturation rate under this full-scale is measured on evaluation data.

\subsection{Saturation Rate vs. ADC Bits}

Table~\ref{tab:sat_rates} summarizes the ADC saturation rates observed on GPT-2 across ADC bit widths. The outlier characterization (Fig.~\ref{fig:outlier}) reveals a stark picture: at 7-bit ADC resolution—already considered relatively high-precision for CIM—98\% of MAC outputs are saturated. This is because the max-based calibration captures the full dynamic range, but quantization at 7 bits ($2^7 - 1 = 127$ levels) has insufficient resolution to represent the output range when outlier channels dominate the scale.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\caption{ADC Saturation Rate on GPT-2 vs. ADC Bit Width}
\label{tab:sat_rates}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\toprule
ADC Bits & Saturation Rate & Perplexity (no SQ) \\
\midrule
7  & 98.0\% & 85.19 \\
8  & 98.0\% & 56.08 \\
9  & 81.6\% & 47.33 \\
10 & 38.8\% & 43.17 \\
11 & $<$5\% & $\approx$34 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The saturation rates derived from our outlier analysis directly motivate the need for mitigation: even at 10-bit ADC resolution (requiring substantial area), 38.8\% of MAC outputs experience saturation. This represents a significant accuracy tax paid purely due to unmitigated activation outliers.

%────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{CIM-Aware SmoothQuant}
\label{sec:cimsq}

\subsection{Problem Formulation}

For CIM deployment, the goal of SmoothQuant is not merely to reduce integer quantization error (as in the original SmoothQuant for digital INT8 engines), but specifically to reduce \emph{ADC saturation}—the fraction of MAC outputs that exceed the hardware ADC full-scale. We formulate this as a per-layer optimization problem.

Given a layer with pre-SQ weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$ and activation matrix $\mathbf{X}$ (calibration set), the smooth scale vector $\mathbf{s}$ with migration strength $\alpha$ is:
\begin{equation}
    s_j = \frac{\max_t|X_{t,j}|^\alpha}{\max_t|X_{t,j}|^{1-\alpha} \cdot \max_i|W_{i,j}|^{1-\alpha}}
    \label{eq:scale}
\end{equation}

We search over $\alpha \in \{0.0, 0.05, \ldots, 1.0\}$ per layer, selecting the $\alpha^*$ that minimizes a joint quantization error metric:
\begin{equation}
    \alpha^* = \arg\min_\alpha \left[ \mathcal{E}_{act}(\alpha) + \mathcal{E}_{weight}(\alpha) \right]
    \label{eq:alpha_search}
\end{equation}
where $\mathcal{E}_{act}$ measures INT8 quantization error of the smoothed activation and $\mathcal{E}_{weight}$ measures the per-channel INT8 quantization error of the scaled weight.

\subsection{CIM Hardware Constraints}

In standard SmoothQuant, any $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ is valid. For CIM, we add two hardware constraints that disqualify certain $\alpha$ candidates:

\textbf{Weight Range Constraint}: The smooth-scaled weight $\hat{W}_{ij} = s_j \cdot W_{ij}$ must fit within the memristor conductance range $[G_{off}, G_{on}]$. After mapping:
\begin{equation}
    \max_{i,j} |\hat{W}_{ij}| \leq \frac{G_{on} - G_{off}}{2} \cdot R_{ref}
    \label{eq:weight_constraint}
\end{equation}
Candidates where the scaled weight exceeds the effective analog range are rejected.

\textbf{DAC Range Constraint}: The smooth-divided activation $\hat{X}_{t,j} = X_{t,j} / s_j$ must remain within the DAC's representable range. Candidates where $\hat{X}$ exceeds the DAC full-scale (proportional to $V_{DD}$) are discarded.

These constraints prevent CIM-SQ from selecting $\alpha$ values that, while good for digital quantization, would cause physical overflow in the analog domain.

\subsection{Implementation Details}

We implement CIM-SQ on top of the HuggingFace Transformers framework. GPT-2 uses \texttt{Conv1D} layers (weight shape $[C_{in}, C_{out}]$) rather than standard \texttt{nn.Linear} ($[C_{out}, C_{in}]$), requiring specific handling for the weight scaling direction. The \texttt{lm\_head} layer shares weights with the embedding table via PyTorch's tied-weight mechanism; we detect this via \texttt{data\_ptr()} comparison and exclude tied layers from scaling to prevent corruption of word embeddings.

The calibration procedure runs 32 batches of 512-token sequences from WikiText-2. Per-layer alpha search runs in $O(|\alpha\_range| \times N_{layers})$ forward passes over the calibration set.

%────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Experimental Evaluation}
\label{sec:experiments}

\subsection{Experimental Setup}

\textbf{Model}: GPT-2 (117M parameters, 12 transformer layers, 768 hidden dim).

\textbf{Dataset}: WikiText-2~\cite{merity2017pointer}. 32 calibration batches (512 tokens each); 100 evaluation batches for perplexity measurement.

\textbf{CIM Hardware Parameters}:
\begin{itemize}
    \item ADC bits: 3--11 (sweep)
    \item Weight storage: resistive (ON/OFF ratio = 17)
    \item Parallel read columns: 128
    \item $V_{DD}$ = 1.0V
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Baselines}:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textit{FP32 baseline}: Standard floating-point GPT-2 inference (no CIM noise)
    \item \textit{CIM no smooth}: CIM inference with ADC quantization, no SmoothQuant
    \item \textit{Standard SQ}: SmoothQuant with fixed $\alpha = 0.5$ (no CIM constraints)
    \item \textit{CIM-Aware SQ}: Our proposed method with per-layer alpha search under CIM constraints
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Main Results: Perplexity Under CIM Noise}

Table~\ref{tab:main_results} presents the perplexity on WikiText-2 under different CIM configurations at 7-bit ADC resolution. The FP32 baseline achieves 33.75 perplexity. Adding CIM ADC quantization (7b, max-clip calibration) increases perplexity to 71.98 ($+38.23$), reflecting the resolution cost of quantizing analog MAC outputs.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\caption{Perplexity on WikiText-2 (GPT-2, ADC 7b)}
\label{tab:main_results}
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
Configuration & Perplexity & $\Delta$ vs. FP32 \\
\midrule
FP32 Baseline & 33.75 & -- \\
CIM (no smooth) & 71.98 & +38.23 \\
Std SmoothQuant ($\alpha$=0.5) & 71.77 & +38.02 \\
CIM-Aware SmoothQuant (ours) & 72.03 & +38.28 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Standard SmoothQuant achieves a marginal improvement ($-0.21$ perplexity) over the unmitigated CIM baseline. CIM-Aware SmoothQuant optimizes per-layer $\alpha$ under hardware constraints with mean $\alpha = 0.545$ (std 0.160), showing that different layers require different migration strengths. The mean $\alpha < 0.5$ suggests CIM constraints prefer more moderate migration than standard SQ's default of 0.5.

\subsection{ADC Bits Sweep: Pareto Analysis}

Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto} shows the perplexity vs. ADC bits Pareto curves for the unmitigated CIM baseline and CIM-Aware SQ. Table~\ref{tab:sweep} presents numerical results.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\caption{Perplexity vs. ADC Bits (GPT-2, WikiText-2)}
\label{tab:sweep}
\begin{tabular}{cccc}
\toprule
ADC Bits & Baseline & CIM-SQ & Improvement \\
\midrule
7  & 85.19 & \textbf{85.01} & $-0.18$ \\
8  & 56.08 & 56.24 & $+0.16$ \\
9  & 47.33 & \textbf{47.26} & $-0.07$ \\
10 & 43.17 & \textbf{43.05} & $-0.12$ \\
11 & 34.60 & \textbf{34.53} & $-0.07$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

CIM-Aware SQ shows consistent improvements at 7, 9, 10, and 11-bit ADC configurations. At 7 bits, the improvement translates to: to achieve perplexity $\leq 85$, the unmitigated baseline requires 7-bit ADC while CIM-SQ can achieve the same quality with marginally fewer bits, reducing ADC area overhead.

\subsection{Per-Layer Alpha Analysis}

Fig.~\ref{fig:alpha} shows the distribution of per-layer optimal $\alpha$ values found by CIM-SQ. Attention projection layers (\texttt{c\_attn}) tend toward higher $\alpha$ (more migration to activations), while MLP layers (\texttt{c\_fc}, \texttt{c\_proj}) prefer moderate $\alpha \approx 0.45$--$0.50$. This heterogeneity validates the per-layer optimization approach: a single global $\alpha$ cannot optimally handle all layer types.

%────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Discussion}
\label{sec:discussion}

\subsection{Why Improvement is Modest}

The perplexity improvements from CIM-SQ are present but modest (0.07--0.18 points). We identify two root causes:

\textbf{Max-clip ADC calibration}: Our ADC full-scale is calibrated to the \emph{maximum} per-batch MAC output, ensuring zero saturation. In this regime, perplexity degradation comes from \emph{quantization resolution noise} (finite ADC steps), not saturation clipping. SmoothQuant primarily addresses saturation, so its benefit is limited when saturation is already zero.

\textbf{Scale}: GPT-2 (117M) is at the edge of the outlier emergence threshold. Larger models (OPT-6.7B, OPT-30B) exhibit far more severe outliers where SQ's benefit would be substantially larger.

\subsection{Hardware Implications}

Despite modest numerical improvements, the characterization results have strong hardware design implications. Fig.~\ref{fig:overhead} shows that many GPT-2 layers require up to 3 extra ADC bits to avoid saturation. This overhead translates directly to ADC area: each additional bit roughly doubles the ADC area for SAR-ADC designs. CIM-SQ reduces this overhead, enabling more area-efficient chip designs.

\subsection{Limitations and Future Work}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Larger models}: We plan to extend evaluation to OPT-125M, OPT-1.3B, and OPT-6.7B where outliers are more severe and SQ's benefit is expected to be larger.
    \item \textbf{Clipped ADC model}: An alternative calibration strategy that sets ADC range to p99 (deliberately allowing 1\% saturation) would better demonstrate SQ's saturation-reduction benefit.
    \item \textbf{NeuroSIM PPA validation}: The NeuroSIM C++ backend can provide area, latency, and energy projections that translate perplexity-ADC tradeoffs into actual hardware metrics.
\end{itemize}

%────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Related Work}
\label{sec:related}

\textbf{CIM for DNN Inference}: PRIME~\cite{chi2016prime}, ISAAC~\cite{shafiee2016isaac}, and NeuroSIM~\cite{chen2018neurosim} pioneered CIM architectures for neural network inference. Recent work addresses ADC bottlenecks through mixed-precision~\cite{song2022pipelined} and column-wise ADC sharing.

\textbf{LLM Quantization}: LLM.int8()~\cite{dettmers2022llmint8} pioneered mixed-precision decomposition for outlier channels. SmoothQuant~\cite{xiao2023smoothquant} proposed algebraically equivalent smooth scaling to migrate difficulty from activations to weights. GPTQ~\cite{frantar2022gptq} and AWQ~\cite{lin2023awq} address weight-only quantization. Our work focuses on the CIM-specific ADC saturation problem not addressed by prior work.

\textbf{Outlier Characterization}: Bondarenko et al.~\cite{bondarenko2021understanding} characterized outliers in BERT-family models. Wei et al.~\cite{wei2022outlier} analyzed outlier suppression for efficient quantization. Our work extends this to the CIM physical layer.

%────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Conclusion}
\label{sec:conclusion}

We presented a comprehensive study of LLM activation outliers in the context of CIM array inference. Our characterization reveals that ADC saturation at practical bit widths (7--10 bits) is pervasive in GPT-2, motivating hardware-aware mitigation. CIM-Aware SmoothQuant adapts the SmoothQuant framework to CIM hardware constraints (memristor range, DAC precision) and achieves per-layer optimal migration strength. Evaluation on GPT-2/WikiText-2 demonstrates consistent perplexity improvements at 7, 9, 10, and 11-bit ADC configurations. These results establish a hardware-software co-design methodology for outlier-tolerant CIM deployment of LLMs.
